turns.content: I've read about the difference between a Mutex and an RwLock in Rust but I still don't fully understand when to use one versus the other, especially regarding concurrency patterns.

Think of it like a library: if you need exclusive access to a book so only one person can read it at a time, that's where a Mutex<T> shines because it ensures mutual exclusion. However, if many readers need to view the same document simultaneously without blocking each other, an RwLock is better since it allows multiple concurrent reads while still protecting writes.
That analogy helps but I'm concerned about what happens when a thread holds a lock forever; does Rust's borrow checker or runtime handle that automatically?

Rust doesn't allow deadlocks at compile time, yet you can still get stuck if your code logic is flawed. The real danger comes from lock poisoning where a poisoned RwLock prevents new readers from acquiring the lock until it's reset by calling drop or unlock manually.
I've been using std::thread for my background workers, but I'm confused about when to use Mutex versus RwLock. Can you explain the difference?

Think of it like a coffee shop counter: a single server can only serve one person at a time with a standard mutex, whereas if most people are just reading the menu board (read-heavy), using an rwlock allows multiple people to look at it simultaneously without blocking each other. In Rust terms, Mutex<T> guarantees exclusive access, meaning only one thread holds the lock while RwLock<T> permits concurrent reads with exclusive writes.
That makes sense for general usage, but what happens when a thread gets stuck on a lock? I've heard about poison and do you think that's common enough to worry about?

When a panicked thread holds a Mutex or RwLock, it poisons the lock so other threads can't steal its resources; this is why you should avoid panicking while holding a lock. For production code where panic-free behavior matters, many teams switch to parking_lot::Mutex and parking_lot::RwLock instead of std's versions since they don't poison on panic.
